behaviors.description: The review input is scoped to the lines that changed, not the full content of each modified file.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: code analyzes the entire file instead of just the diff lines. A Unit test of diff parsing logic alone could pass even if the GitHub API integration is broken (e.g., if the code requests the full file instead of the diff). An Integration test that mocks the GitHub API with a realistic diff payload (including surrounding context) and verifies that only changed lines are extracted and analyzed is necessary to catch this integration failure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the GitHub API to return a realistic pull request diff with full file context. Construct a scenario where a 200-line file has 5 lines changed. Verify that: (1) the analysis extracts exactly the 5 changed lines, not the full 200-line file; (2) when findings are identified, they're about those 5 lines, not the surrounding context.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test of the diff parser wouldn't catch if the integration with the GitHub API client requests the whole file. A System test against real GitHub would work but is overkill. Integration is the minimum level that catches the real failure.

behaviors.description: Inline comments are attached to the correct positions in the diff view on the pull request.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: comments are posted to wrong line numbers, or the position calculations don't match GitHub's diff position format, causing posts to fail or appear on wrong lines. An Integration test with a mocked GitHub API could verify position calculation logic, but wouldn't catch failures that only occur when posting to real GitHub (position out of bounds, format mismatches, or GitHub's specific line-numbering rules for diffs). GitHub's diff position numbering has non-obvious rules about how additions, deletions, and context interact. A System test that actually posts to real GitHub (or a test server that fully simulates it) is necessary to verify positions are valid and comments appear correctly.
behaviors.test_description: Run the automation against a real (or test-server-simulated) GitHub pull request. Create a PR that modifies multiple sections of a file with insertions and deletions mixed with context lines. Verify that each inline comment appears on the correct line in the GitHub diff view by checking the actual UI/comment placement, not just that the POST request succeeded.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Mocking the GitHub API comment endpoint would hide position errors that GitHub itself would reject. The key failure—'GitHub rejects my position as out of bounds'—can only be caught by posting to real GitHub or a fully faithful mock.

behaviors.description: The agent identifies at least one real defect in a pull request that contains a known introduced bug.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the LLM fails to identify a known defect in the code. This depends entirely on what a real LLM produces, not on GitHub API mechanics or diff formatting. A Unit or Integration test with a mocked LLM (returning canned responses) would always pass and wouldn't catch the real failure: the LLM genuinely misses the bug. An Agentic test that calls a real LLM with a code diff containing a known introduced bug (e.g., missing null check, unreachable error handler) is necessary to verify the identification capability.
behaviors.test_description: Construct a code snippet with a specific, known introduced bug (e.g., using an uninitialized variable, missing bounds check, or uncaught exception path). Format it as a pull request diff. Call the LLM with this diff and the agent's analysis prompt. Verify that the returned findings include the known bug (or a detection of the problematic pattern). Repeat with multiple bug types to confirm the LLM's capability.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A mocked LLM would hide the real failure ('the model didn't identify the bug'). A System test running the full automation would also work, but the minimum level to catch the failure is Agentic—you only need to call the LLM, not post to GitHub.

behaviors.description: Every finding listed in the top-level summary corresponds to an inline comment that was also posted — no findings appear in the summary without a matching inline comment.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the summary includes findings that were never posted as inline comments (e.g., a finding is dropped during comment posting, or the summary includes duplicates/ghosts). A Unit test of just the correspondence logic would miss failures in how findings are collected, filtered, or processed during the full flow. An Integration test that mocks the LLM to return a controlled set of findings, captures all posted comments (inline and summary), and verifies one-to-one correspondence is necessary to catch integration failures between finding generation and posting.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return a specific, controlled set of findings (e.g., 3 bugs, 2 style issues). Capture all GitHub API calls to post comments. For each inline comment posted, verify that the finding appears in the summary comment. For each finding in the summary, verify that an inline comment exists. Verify that no extra findings appear in the summary and no posted inline comments are 'orphaned'.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test of correspondence logic alone wouldn't catch if findings are lost during comment posting or if the summary generation logic is broken. Integration is the minimum to test the full finding-to-comment pipeline.

behaviors.description: The full sequence — reading the diff, posting inline comments, posting the summary comment — completes correctly for a real pull request without manual intervention.
behaviors.justification: The failure mode is: the automation fails at any point in the real-world end-to-end flow (network errors, API format changes, GitHub rejects positions, LLM is unavailable, etc.). An Integration test with mocks would pass even if the real GitHub API or LLM behaves differently. A System test that runs the full automation against a real pull request (with real GitHub API calls and real LLM inference) is necessary to catch failures that only occur in production-like conditions: rate limits, unexpected response formats, or timing issues.
behaviors.test_description: Create a test pull request in a real GitHub repository (or test environment with GitHub-like API). Run the full review automation against it: read the diff from the real GitHub API, call the real LLM to analyze the code, post inline comments to the real PR, and post a summary comment. Verify that all steps complete without errors, no comments are silently dropped, and the PR shows all comments correctly in the UI. Run the test multiple times to verify non-deterministic LLM behavior doesn't break the flow.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration with mocks would hide real failures like GitHub rejecting the POST format or the LLM API timing out. System level is necessary to verify the automation handles real GitHub and LLM infrastructure correctly.
